rationale: A PowerShell process using an encoded command spawned cmd.exe to add a Run key persistence entry ('C:\Path\[x].exe') via reg.exe (A0, EV-001, EV-002), which is a classic persistence technique. However, approximately 4 seconds later, a second PowerShell instance (with a different encoded command containing '-Cleanup' semantics) spawned cmd.exe to delete the same Run key entry (EV-003, EV-004, EV-005), suggesting this may be a post-exploitation framework (e.g., a C2 agent or red-team tool) performing a test-and-cleanup persistence check. The obfuscated encoded PowerShell parent commands, execution from the Administrator Temp directory, and the '[x]' placeholder-style naming are all indicators of a framework or tool rather than legitimate admin activity. The registry change was ultimately reverted, so no confirmed persistent implant remains, but the underlying tooling and behavior pattern warrant investigation — the encoded commands and the binary 'C:\Path\[x].exe' have not been analyzed.
